coredata.dc:title: Characterization of green-synthesized carbon quantum dots from spent coffee grounds for EDLC electrode applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Arora, Grishika
Sabran, Nuur Syahidah
Liew, Chiam-Wen
Ng, Chai Yan
Low, Foo Wah
Singh, Pramod K.
Jun, Hieng Kiat
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the green synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from spent coffee grounds using a hydrothermal method, offering an eco-friendly, cost-effective, and straightforward approach to nanomaterial production. The synthesized CQDs, with particle sizes ranging from 1.6 to 4.4 nm, exhibited notable fluorescence, achieving quantum yields of 37.0 %, 54.3 %, and 63.3 % depending on the coffee source. Characterization technique, including XRD, FTIR, SEM, TEM, and BET, confirmed their structural suitability of these CQDs for energy storage applications. Their electrochemical performance was evaluated through cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Among the CQDs tested, those derived from spent Liberica coffee ground (medium roasted) demonstrated superior performance, with a discharging specific capacitance of 97.5 F/g, an energy density of 4.3 Wh/kg, and a power density of 130.6 W/kg at a current density of 0.5 A/g. Additionally, they exhibited acceptable internal resistance (R a = 0.01 kΩ and R ab = 16.9 kΩ), indicating favourable charge transfer characteristics. These results underscore the enhanced energy storage potential of CQDs derived from spent coffee grounds. The findings not only highlight the excellent electrochemical performance but also support the viability of biomass waste as a valuable resource for advanced energy storage applications, promoting sustainable, eco-friendly technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spent coffee grounds
Carbon quantum dots
Photoluminescence
Green synthesis

Nuur Syahidah Sabran thanks Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman for the provision of the Post-doctoral Research Scholarship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from spent coffee grounds.

(a) Absorption spectra of the synthesized CQDs; (b) PL spectra recorded for CQDs synthesized from medium roasted imported Arabica SCGs; (c) PL spectra recorded for CQDs synthesized from medium roasted local Arabica SCGs; (d) PL spectra recorded for CQDs synthesized from medium roasted local Liberica SCGs.
(a) XRD spectra of CQDs synthesized from three different spent coffee grounds; (b) FTIR spectra of raw spent coffee ground and CQDs synthesized from Liberica medium spent coffee grounds.

SEM image of CQDs sythesized from (a) medium roasted imported Arabica SCGs; (b) medium roasted local Arabica SCGs; (c) medium roasted local Liberica SCGs; (d) TEM image of CQDs synthesized from medium roasted local Liberica SCGs; (e) SAED of CQDs synthesized from medium roasted local Liberica SCGs.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of carbon electrode samples with and without CQDs synthesized from medium roasted local Liberica SCGs.

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of CQDs synthesized from (a) medium roasted imported Arabica SCGs; (b) medium roasted local Arabica SCGs, and (c) medium roasted local Liberica SCGs; (d) Specific capacitance vs scan rate of the different synthesized CQDs samples.

GCD curves of CQDs synthesized from (a) medium roasted local Liberica SGCs; (b) medium roasted local Arabica SCGs, and (c) medium roasted imported Arabica SCGs; (d) EIS curves of all the three brands.
Quantum yield percentages of synthesized CQDs from different coffee brands.
Comparison of Liberica medium CQD FTIR with Calotropis gigantea.
Surface texture properties and specific capacitance of carbon electrode with and without CQDs.
Comparison of supercapacitor performances with various CQDs sources.

head.ce:title: Characterization of green-synthesized carbon quantum dots from spent coffee grounds for EDLC electrode applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the green synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from spent coffee grounds using a hydrothermal method, offering an eco-friendly, cost-effective, and straightforward approach to nanomaterial production. The synthesized CQDs, with particle sizes ranging from 1.6 to 4.4 nm, exhibited notable fluorescence, achieving quantum yields of 37.0 %, 54.3 %, and 63.3 % depending on the coffee source. Characterization technique, including XRD, FTIR, SEM, TEM, and BET, confirmed their structural suitability of these CQDs for energy storage applications. Their electrochemical performance was evaluated through cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Among the CQDs tested, those derived from spent Liberica coffee ground (medium roasted) demonstrated superior performance, with a discharging specific capacitance of 97.5 F/g, an energy density of 4.3 Wh/kg, and a power density of 130.6 W/kg at a current density of 0.5 A/g. Additionally, they exhibited acceptable internal resistance (R a = 0.01 kΩ and R ab = 16.9 kΩ), indicating favourable charge transfer characteristics. These results underscore the enhanced energy storage potential of CQDs derived from spent coffee grounds. The findings not only highlight the excellent electrochemical performance but also support the viability of biomass waste as a valuable resource for advanced energy storage applications, promoting sustainable, eco-friendly technologies.
Synthesis of CQD from spent coffee grounds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing desire for modern lifestyles and the increasing global population have led to a significant rise in energy demand. This has accelerated the need for high-performance energy storage devices. Among the most common energy storage systems are batteries and electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs), also known as supercapacitors. EDLCs operate based on the electrostatic interactions of electrodes with a large specific surface area. While they offer exceptionally high power densities (PD), their energy densities (ED) are often considerably lower compared to metal-ion batteries. Consequently, there is a strong interest in research aimed at enhancing the energy densities of supercapacitors to improve their overall performance [1–4].

Researchers have explored various materials to enhance supercapacitor performance, including metal oxides [5], graphene [6], carbon nanotubes [7], conductive polymers [8], metal-organic frameworks-derived porous carbon [9], and doped porous carbon [10]. Carbon-based materials are particularly attractive for supercapacitor electrodes due to their excellent conductivity, physicochemical stability, affordability, and abundant availability. These materials typically exhibit EDLC-type energy storage behaviour, characterized by efficient ion adsorption and desorption kinetics, largely influenced by their porous structure and high specific surface area [11]. Recently, Pallavolu et al. [12] synthesized CQDs and N-/S-doped CQDs encapsulated in graphene sheets using lemon juice. Their symmetric supercapacitor (S-CQDs//S-CQDs) demonstrated impressive specific capacitance, specific energy, and power densities, with values of 55.7 F/g, 15.2 Wh/kg, and 700 W/kg, respectively at 1 A/g.

Nanotechnology has a profound impact on the world, with nanoscale materials offering significant advantages in various industries. Quantum dots (QDs), carbon nanotubes, and fullerene derivatives have become essential in multiple fields [13]. Carbon Quantum Dots CQDs are nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm, renowned for their unique optical properties and valuable applications in optoelectronic systems, energy storage devices, and medical diagnostics [14]. On a bulk semiconductor substrate, QDs have high conduction states within the valence band. Energy input, such as photon absorption, can elevate electrons to this high state. Typically, when electrons return to the valence band, they emit light through a process called luminescence, with the emitted photons having energy corresponding to the semiconductor's band gap. As particle size approaches the Bohr radius, energy levels transition from continuous to discrete, and a phenomenon known as the quantum confinement effect. QDs, being extremely small semiconductor nanoparticles, exhibit quantized electronic energies similar to those of atoms and organic fluorophores [15].

Since the introduction of CQDs in the early 2000s, extensive research has focused on their remarkable luminescent properties, high solubility, and biocompatibility. These materials also possess exceptional physicochemical properties and wavelength-dependent photoluminescence [16]. Graphene oxide, graphite, and other higher-dimensional carbon-based materials are used in the top-down approach to produce carbon-based nanomaterials through chemical or physical methods such as arc discharge, chemical oxidation, chemical exfoliation, laser ablation, and ultrasonication [17–20]. In contrast, the bottom-up approach involves creating carbon dots from smaller carbon-based molecules using methods such as pyrolysis, microwave, and hydrothermal processes [21–24]. Ren et al. [25] used low-cost biomass residue as a carbon precursor to prepare biomass-derived CQDs with high surface activity via pyrolysis. They observed significant differences in luminescence properties and fluorescence behaviours at different pyrolysis temperatures. The CQDs demonstrated superior surface activity, and the styrene-in-water Pickering emulsion stabilized by biomass-derived CQDs showed high stability, with emulsions made with CQD400 and CQD500 exhibiting no stratification after 72 h. This research provides a simple method for mass-producing nanosized surfactants with broad application potential. Zhou et al. [26] synthesized CQDs by adding 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium perchlorate to a three-electrode system, which included a carbon paper working electrode with chemically vapor-deposited MWCNTs, a platinum wire counter electrode, and an Ag/AgClO4 reference electrode in a degassed acetonitrile solution. They produced deep-blue luminescent CQDs from MWCNTs and developed a similar electrochemical method to convert MWCNTs into highly efficient luminescent carbon nanocrystals, which can be easily dispersed in various solvents.

The hydrothermal technique is highly recommended for producing high-quality, environmentally friendly, and rapidly prepared carbon products [26–29]. Frequent use of toxic and harmful chemicals in the production of the CQDs has led to growing interest in using biomass waste materials for this purpose, driven by environmental and financial considerations. Biomass waste—derived from food scraps, animal waste, and agricultural residues—is abundant and inexpensive, yet it is often overlooked as a valuable resource. Spent coffee grounds (SCGs), for example, are a by-product of coffee brewing and are produced in large quantities due to the widespread consumption of coffee worldwide. This presents a unique opportunity to repurpose SCGs into valuable carbon-based nanomaterials, reducing environmental impact while promoting a zero-waste economy.

SCGs contain a variety of potentially bioactive substances, including polysaccharides, proteins, phenolics, melanoidins, and dietary fiber (DF). Additional fibers in SCGs include mannooligosaccharides (classified as Food for Specified Health Uses), resistant starch, and oligosaccharides. Producing CQDs from biomass can be a chemical-free process that aims to convert waste into an inexpensive, renewable, value-added product with commercial scalability. While refining carbon compounds from crude biomass is time-consuming, the recent interest in biomass-derived CQDs highlights their cost-effectiveness and sustainability benefits [30]. A growing interest has been observed in hydrothermal processes, including carbonization, liquefaction, and gasification, for producing hydrochar, bio-oil, and gas, with hydrothermal carbonization demonstrating particular efficiency [31].

Our study introduces a novel one-step hydrothermal method for synthesizing CQDs from SCGs, specifically chosen for their availability, affordability, and sustainability. Compared to other biomass waste options, SCGs offer several advantages: they are a readily accessible by-product of one of the most widely consumed beverages in the world, and their reuse contributes to reducing waste from the coffee industry, aligning with global efforts toward a circular economy. Furthermore, SCGs are rich in carbon content and contain various functional groups that facilitate the formation of CQDs, making them an ideal precursor for carbon-based nanomaterials.

This study focuses on three coffee varieties— medium roasted local Arabica SCGs, medium roasted imported Arabica SCGs, and medium roasted local Liberica SCGs—to explore how the biomass precursor impacts the performance of CQDs in energy storage applications. By utilizing SCGs, this research not only provides new insights into enhancing the performance of supercapacitor electrodes but also underscores the broader potential of upcycling biomass waste for sustainable energy solutions. This approach could pave the way for environmentally friendly technologies that contribute to the development of high-performance energy storage devices.

Spent coffee grounds were collected from consumers as biomass waste. Three types of coffee grounds were used in this study, namely single-origin Arabica coffee grounds with beans grown in the highlands of east Malaysia (Sabah), single-origin Liberica coffee grounds with beans grown in southern Malaysia (Johor), and an imported commercial blend of Arabica coffee grounds. All coffee beans were medium roasted and ground into a fine powder before consumption. It is noted that the roasting time is not specified by the producers, as the beans were procured commercially. This lack of detail limits our ability to directly correlate roasting time with CQD size in the current study. No purification or pre-treatment was performed to the spent coffee grounds. No other chemical reagents were used except for ethanol, which was used for cleaning purposes.

The synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from spent coffee grounds (SCGs) was carried out using a hydrothermal method, which is known for its eco-friendly approach and ability to produce CQDs with desirable properties for energy storage applications. Specifically, 2 g of spent coffee grounds were first dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water. This mixture was stirred continuously at a controlled temperature of 70 °C for three hours to ensure thorough dispersion and extraction of the organic content from the SCGs. Following this, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave, a sealed vessel capable of withstanding high temperatures and pressures, which is essential for the hydrothermal reaction. The autoclave was then heated to 200 °C and maintained at this temperature for four hours. The elevated temperature and pressure inside the autoclave facilitated the breakdown of the biomass into carbon-rich quantum dots. This process also promotes the formation of functional groups on the CQD surfaces, which enhances their optical and electrochemical properties. Once the hydrothermal process was complete, the resulting solution was cooled down to room temperature and filtered to remove any remaining solid particles. To purify the CQDs, the solution was subjected to dialysis using a 12,000 Da dialysis tubing sack. This step, carried out for 48 h, helped to remove any unwanted small molecules or impurities, ensuring that only the CQDs remained in the solution. The same synthesis procedure was repeated for other types of spent coffee grounds to compare their properties. By employing the hydrothermal synthesis method, the CQDs produced exhibited excellent dispersibility in water and other solvents, along with promising electrochemical characteristics. Fig. 1 provides a schematic representation of this synthesis process, highlighting the critical steps from raw material preparation to CQD purification. This method's simplicity and scalability make it a viable approach for producing high-quality CQDs from a renewable and abundant resource like spent coffee grounds.

UV–Vis spectroscopy was conducted using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer model 100 Conc Cary, recording absorption spectra over the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed on the CQDs to examine their optical properties and understand quantum confinement effects. The characterization was conducted using FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments). The structural properties of the CQDs were analyzed using a Shimadzu LabX XRD-6000 X-ray diffractometer, equipped with a monochromatic Cu-Kα radiation source (λ = 1.54056 Å). Measurements were taken over a 2θ range from 10° to 80° with a step size of 10° FTIR analysis was carried out using a Nicolet iS10 FTIR Spectrum, with OMNIC 8 software, equipped with an ATR internal reflection system, covering a wavenumber range from 4000 to 400 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The surface topography of the composite specimen was investigated using a Hitachi S-3400 N SEM. High-resolution TEM (TEM) images were obtained to examine the fine structural details of the CQDs using a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis was conducted using a Tristar II series 3020 to examine the surface area and porosity of the CQDs. The capacitive behavior of the EDLC was investigated using cyclic voltammetry (CV) at different scan rates. Charge-discharge characteristics were determined through galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to assess internal resistance. All measurements were conducted using a ZIVE SPI potentiostat, with the EDLC device securely mounted in a sample holder. The synthesis process was replicated to assess the reproducibility of the CQDs. Although the deviation observed in PL is not much (less than 5 %), only the optimum or best results are presented in this work. This is also applicable to the characterization of energy storage devices.

Fig. 2 shows the absorbance profiles of medium roasted local Liberica SCGs, medium roasted local Arabica SCGs, and medium roasted imported Arabica SCGs (for brevity, these samples are also referred as Liberica medium, Arabica local and Arabica medium, respectively in the plots and discussions). The absorption bands in Fig. 2 (a) in the 300–350 nm region represent the π–π* transitions of the C = C double bond and the related electron transitions in oxygen-containing CQDs [32]. The absorption peaks observed in the UV region indicate the formation of CQDs, with an extension into the visible range seen in the spectral tail. The highest absorbance values were recorded at specific wavelengths (nm): 3.03 at 334 nm for Arabica medium, 2.84 at 325 nm for Arabica local, and 2.2 at 345 nm for Liberica medium. These wavelength peaks indicate significant absorption properties, suggesting reduced susceptibility to fading in colored materials. The relatively low absorbance values suggest a limited number of molecules available for interaction with light [33]. The specific absorption properties suggest that the CQDs can be tailored for specific energy applications, potentially making them valuable in areas such as photovoltaic cells where targeted wavelength absorption is advantageous.

Additionally, as shown in Figs. 2 (b)-(d), the excitation wavelength was varied from 350 to 700 nm to thoroughly evaluate the fluorescence properties of the CQDs. Varied excitation wavelengths caused a redshift, with peak intensity observed at 410 nm for both Arabica medium and Liberica medium, while the peak intensity of the Arabica local was at 380 nm. This redshift behaviour may be attributed to the π–π* transition of the graphitic sp2 core at lower excitation energies. The presence of dots of varying sizes in the CQDs and the observed shift implied multiple emission trap states [34]. These findings suggested enhanced emission and absorption characteristics in the CQDs. The quantum yield (QY) of different brands was provided in Table 1 . High QY fluorophores are known for their robust fluorescence, making them valuable in diverse industries such as biology, optics, and energy sectors. The luminous QY of the CQDs is important when considering their suitability as practical luminescent materials. Moreover, a higher quantum yield enhances imaging effects, playing a critical role in evaluating the optical performance of CQDs, particularly in applications like energy [35]. In comparison, Aumugham et al. [36] synthesized CQDs from Catharanthus roseus (white) leaves and achieved a quantum yield of 28.2 % at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. Meanwhile, Jing et al. [37] obtained approximately 40 % solid by-product (hydrochar) through the hydrothermal treatment of glucose. After treating the hydro char with a diluted NaOH/H2O2 solution, they successfully achieved a high CQD yield of 43.8 wt% (based on glucose) at room temperature.

Tang et al. proposed that CQDs exhibit core-shell structures, which can be either graphitic crystalline (rich in sp2) or amorphous (containing a mixture of sp2/sp3), depending on the prevalence of sp2 carbon within the core [34]. To determine the crystal structure and purity phases, the three CQD samples were characterized using the XRD technique, with results as shown in Fig. 3
(a). A previous study noted that pure PVA exhibited a semi-crystalline nature, evidenced by a broad peak at a 2θ value of 29.39°, corresponding to graphitic carbon diffraction.

The XRD analysis of CQDs synthesized from medium roasted local Arabica SCGs, medium roasted imported Arabica SCGs, and medium roasted local Liberica SCGs was conducted to evaluate their phase purity and crystal structure. The XRD patterns of these CQDs, presented in Fig. 3 (a), reveal several well-defined peaks within the 2θ range of 20° to 80° Notably, the characteristic peaks appear at 2θ values of 36.4°, 39.2°, 43.6°, 64.7°, and 77.9°, respectively. The broad diffraction peaks observed between 20° and 30° in 2θ suggest the presence of amorphous carbon, along with residual organic materials [38]. This broad feature indicates that the carbon atoms in the CQDs are arranged in a relatively disordered or unsystematic manner. The amorphous nature is likely influenced by the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots [39]. These results demonstrate both broad diffraction peaks and sharper, more distinct peaks in the diffractogram, indicating a mix of amorphous and crystalline components within the CQDs.

For comparison, similar studies were conducted by Din et al. [38], who synthesized CQDs from chicken feathers. Their XRD pattern displayed characteristic peaks at 2θ values of 32.5°, 37.4°, 43.6°, 63.96°, and 76.98°, along with a broad peak corresponding to amorphous carbon and organic materials between 20° and 30° in 2θ. Similarly, Thulasi et al. [39] synthesized CQDs from vehicle exhaust soot, and their XRD pattern exhibited small diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 32°, 35°, 38°, 42°, and 48° These findings, across different sources of CQDs, highlight the common occurrence of both amorphous and crystalline phases, with the amorphous carbon region frequently appearing between 20° and 30° in 2θ. The differences in peak positions between these studies likely stem from the varying carbon sources and synthesis methods, which influence the crystallinity and structure of the resulting CQDs.

The surface structure and functional groups of the CQD samples, analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy, reveal the presence of several oxygen-containing functional groups [40,41]. Table 2 summarizes the comparison of stretching frequencies observed in the synthesized CQDs with those reported by Sharma et al. [40] during the synthesis of Calotropis gigantea-derived CQDs.

Fig. 4 presents the SEM and TEM images of the synthesized CQDs. Fig. 4 (a) shows the SEM image of CQDs derived from imported Arabica SCGs, displaying a highly porous and fragmented structure. The rough, irregular surface indicates significant carbonization, where organic material breaks down into smaller fragments, creating a high surface area. Fig. 4 (b), depicting CQDs derived from local Arabica SCGs, reveals a more compact and smoother surface with less porosity. This suggests differences in carbonization, likely due to variations in the coffee beans' composition or processing. The more uniform structure may result in lower surface area but improved crystallinity and conductivity, making it suitable for electronic or optoelectronic applications. Fig. 4 (c) shows the CQDs sample synthesized from local Liberica SCGs. It has the most compact and dense structure among the three samples. The layered, tightly packed morphology suggests a strong carbon matrix with minimal porosity, which may offer lower surface area but higher mechanical stability and conductivity. This makes it ideal for durable materials, such as electrodes in energy storage or conductive films.

TEM is utilized to determine the size, nanocrystalline structure, shape, and interplanar spacing of the produced CQDs, proving to be an invaluable tool in materials research. The TEM image of Liberica medium CQDs ( Fig. 4 (d)) shows they are individually isolated and uniformly dispersed without significant aggregation. The spacing between the planes of carbon atoms, calculated to be 0.2676 nm by TEM, aligns with the XRD results. The average size of the CQDs ranged from 1.6 to 4.4 nm [42–43]. In comparison with the work of Arumugham et al. [36] where they synthesized CQDs from Catharanthus roseus, resulting in spherical, well-dispersed particles with an average size of around 5 nm. Similarly, Inayat et al. [4] synthesized CQDs from tea leaves, obtaining a uniform size distribution of 2–5 nm, with distinct lattice fringes and typical carbon d-spacing values. The studies demonstrated the successful synthesis of CQDs in sufficient quantities, exhibiting spherical morphology and crystalline structure. The SAED pattern shown in Fig. 4 (e) indicates the amorphous nature of the CQDs, corroborating the amorphous observation from previous XRD readings. Overall, the TEM analysis highlights that the CQDs synthesized from Liberica medium coffee grounds are smaller and uniformly dispersed compared to those produced from Catharanthus roseus.

The adsorption and desorption isotherms of N₂ at 77 K for carbon electrode sample with synthesized CQDs from medium roasted local Liberica and the sample without CQDs are shown in Fig. 5 . The textural properties of the carbonized coffee grounds (CCGs) are summarized in Table 3 , alongside the cumulative pore size distribution of the CCGs. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the synthesized CQDs were analyzed using nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and BJH pore size distribution method. Compared to Ding et al., who synthesized CQDs using flower-like Bi₂O₂CO₃ (BOC) and reported a higher surface area of 17.189 m²/g and a total pore volume of 0.1441 cm³/g [44], our results showed lower values. This discrepancy may be attributed to differences in the biomass type and synthesis method used. Biomass-derived CQDs, like those synthesized from SCGs in our study, tend to exhibit variability in surface area and pore structure depending on the source material and processing conditions.

The surface area and pore size distribution play crucial roles in determining the electrochemical performance of CQDs, particularly for energy storage applications. A higher surface area typically allows for better ion adsorption and facilitates faster charge transfer, enhancing the overall performance of supercapacitors. Similarly, an optimized pore structure promotes ion diffusion, contributing to improved cycling stability and energy storage capacity. In our study the choice of SCGs as a precursor and the use of the hydrothermal synthesis method may have influenced the textural properties of the CQDs, leading in lower surface areas compared to other chemical synthesis route. Further exploration of these factors further could offer valuable insights into optimizing the synthesis process to achieve superior electrochemical properties in biomass-derived CQDs.

CV was conducted to evaluate the performance of the EDLC device using electrodes made from synthesized spent coffee grounds. This technique was employed to analyze the hysteresis loop generated between the applied cyclic sweeping voltage and the resulting current response [45]. Specific capacitance was calculated at various scan rates to analyze the capacitive behaviour of the EDLC device, following the equation provided:
(1)
C
s p
=
1
m · s ·
Δ
v
∫
v 1
v 2
I
( v )
d v

In this equation, the integral part represents the area enclosed in the CV curves, s is the scan rate,
Δ
v
is the potential difference between the initial (v 1) and final (v 2) voltage range, and m is the mass of the active material on a single electrode. Fig. 6 displays the CV curves illustrating the electrochemical performance of CQDs derived from various spent coffee grounds, in conjunction with Super P-activated carbon slurry, within a potential range of 0 to 1 V. The maximum specific capacitance achieved was 3.66 F/g at a scan rate of 5 mV/s for CQDs synthesized from Liberica medium SCGs. This is followed by 3.03 F/g for CQDs from Arabica local SCGs, and 0.95 F/g for CQDs from imported Arabica SCGs. The better specific capacitance of the CQDs derived from Liberica medium grounds is attributed to their effective surface area, which contributes to the stability and performance of electrochemical energy storage devices. In comparison, our previous EDLCs [46] without CQDs recorded a specific capacitance of 0.75 F/g at the same scan rate, showcasing effective charge storage at the electrode-electrolyte interface. Although the values obtained are lower than some previously reported results, it's important to note that this study was primarily focused on establishing an initial proof-of-concept. The aim was to demonstrate the feasibility of using green-synthesized CQDs as electrode materials. We recognize that there is room for improvement, and we are committed to refining the experimental conditions in future work to enhance performance and achieve more competitive results.

EDLC samples were fabricated with the addition of the synthesized CQDs into the electrode. The fabrication of EDLCs is based on our previous work [47]. During the charging and discharging of the EDLC cell, GCD measurements are performed using a constant current density. The specific capacitance of EDLC device using electrodes made from synthesized SCGs CQD was calculated using the formula:
(2)
C
s p
=
i
·
Δ
t
m ·
Δ
V

In this equation, (i) represents the applied current,
(
Δ
t
)
stands for the discharging time, (m) denotes the active mass of both electrodes, and
(
Δ
V
)
indicates the applied voltage across the cell. Fig. 7
a demonstrates that Liberica medium CQD with Super P carbon achieves a specific capacitance of 97.5 F/g, with an energy density of 4.3 Wh/kg and a power density of 130.6 W/kg at 0.5 A/g. In comparison, Fig. 7 b shows Arabica local CQD with Super P carbon, which has a specific capacitance of 75 F/g, an energy density of 3.03 Wh/kg, and a power density of 91.4 W/kg at 0.5 A/g. Fig. 7 c illustrates Arabica medium CQD with Super P carbon, demonstrating a specific capacitance of 69.6 F/g, an energy density of 2.4 Wh/kg, and a power density of 72.8 W/kg at 0.5 A/g. Notably, Inayat et al. [4] reported that CQDs derived from tea leaves achieved a specific capacitance of 302 F/g at 0.5 A/g, with an energy density of 41.9 Wh/kg and a power density of 250 W/kg. These results highlight the varying performance of CQDs based on their source, with Liberica medium CQDs showing significant promise for energy storage applications. The GCD study underscores the potential of CQDs derived from different biomass sources in enhancing the performance of energy storage devices.

Table 4 presents a comparative analysis of specific capacitance and performance metrics among various carbon quantum dot (CQD)-based supercapacitors, highlighting significant variations arising from different sources and synthesis methodologies. Among these sources, tea leaf-derived CQDs stand out with an impressive specific capacitance of 302 F/g, paired with an energy density of 41.9 Wh/kg and a power density of 250 W/kg at 0.5 A/g. In contrast, CQDs synthesized from Kombucha fungus and Sideritis vuralii demonstrate considerably lower specific capacitance values of 15.13 F/g and 8.26 F/g, respectively, both measured at a scanning rate of 200 mV/s. Notably, milk-derived CQDs exhibit a broader range of performance, yielding specific capacitance values of 95 F/g and 40 F/g, depending on current densities of 0.12 A/g and 0.2 A/g. While the CQD-PPy composite shows a significantly lower specific capacitance of 0.24 F/g at 150 mV/s, the CDs/Graphene electrodes achieve moderate performance, recording 24.15 F/g at a scanning rate of 100 mV/s within a potential window of 0–3 V. In comparison, the CQDs synthesized from Liberica medium, Arabica local and Arabica medium coffee grounds in this study exhibit competitive specific capacitance values of 97.5 F/g, 75 F/g, and 69.6 F/g, with corresponding energy densities of 4.3 Wh/kg, 3.03 Wh/kg, and 2.4 Wh/kg, alongside power densities of 130.6 W/kg, 91.4 W/kg, and 72.8 W/kg, respectively, at 0.5 A/g.

These findings underscore the impressive performance of coffee derived CQDs, which do not lag behind other sources. The relatively high capacitance values associated with coffee grounds can be attributed to their unique carbon structure and surface chemistry, which may create favourable conditions for charge storage and electron transport.

In order to understand the effect of the synthesized CQDs to the charge kinetic of the energy storage devices, EIS response of the device was measured. EIS is a useful tool for studying an electrochemical system's charge transport mechanism. The Randles circuit is the most widely recognized electric equivalent circuit in EIS (inset of Fig. 7 d). In this circuit, a parallel combination of charge transfer resistance (R b) and double-layer capacitance (CPE1) represents the semicircle. The impedance feature, which includes a depressed semicircle along with a 45-degree linear pattern, is represented by a constant phase element (CPE2) in series with Rs in the equivalent circuit. For our cell, the internal resistances for Liberica medium, Ra and Rab , are 0.01 kΩ and 16.9 kΩ, respectively. For Arabica local, Ra and Rab are 1.1 kΩ and 32.9 kΩ, respectively, while for Arabica medium, Ra and Rab are 0.53 kΩ and 18.26 kΩ, respectively. In comparison with other studies of electrochemical devices, Manna et al. [53] reported a recombination resistance of 3.9 kΩ in CQD-decorated devices, higher than the 2.4 kΩ observed in the control sample. Similarly, Zhang et al. [54] presented an effective inter-dot resistance of 55 kΩ, a value close to the conductance quantum. Our results for Liberica medium CQDs demonstrate a significant difference in internal resistance, indicating that the biomass source and preparation methods play a crucial role in the electrochemical characteristics of CQDs. Nevertheless, our findings highlight the significant potential of Liberica medium CQDs in improving the performance of energy storage devices, positioning them as a promising alternative for future applications.

While this study primarily focuses on establishing a foundational understanding of the charge storage potential of the as-prepared CQDs, the need for further investigations into aspects such as cycling stability and charge-separation to fully comprehend the charge storage dynamics is acknowledged. Separate research is already underway to address these critical factors, with the aim of providing a more comprehensive evaluation of the material's performance. Future work will also aim to incorporate a comprehensive techno-economic analysis to assess the viability of large-scale CQD production, drawing on relevant studies to provide insights into the total capital investment, production costs, and potential profitability of this approach.

In conclusion, this study successfully demonstrates the green synthesis of CQDs from spent coffee grounds (SCGs) using a one-step, eco-friendly hydrothermal method, offering a sustainable and cost-effective solution for energy storage applications. The CQDs exhibited remarkable fluorescence under UV light, achieving quantum yields of 37.0 %, 54.3 %, and 63.3 % for medium roasted local Liberica SCGs, medium roasted local Arabica SCGs, and medium roasted imported Arabica SCGs, respectively. Characterization through HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, SEM, TEM, and BET confirmed their near-spherical shape with sizes ranging from 1.6 to 4.4 nm, and revealed a predominantly crystalline structure, suitable for electrochemical applications. CQDs derived from medium roasted local Liberica SCGs displayed superior electrochemical performance, with a specific capacitance of 97.5 F/g, an energy density of 4.3 Wh/kg, and a power density of 130.6 W/kg at 0.5 A/g, outperforming other variants. Their acceptable internal resistance (Ra = 0.01 kΩ and Rab = 16.9 kΩ) further emphasizes their good charge transfer capabilities, making them a suitable candidate for energy storage device applications. This study highlights the significant potential of biomass waste, particularly spent coffee grounds, as a sustainable and high-performance material for energy storage. The superior energy storage capacity of the CQDs supports the broader shift toward eco-friendly technology, transforming waste into valuable, high-performance energy solutions. The simplicity and cost-effectiveness of the synthesis method in this work position CQDs as a promising component for next-generation energy storage devices, advancing the development of greener, more sustainable technologies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research data policy and data availability statements
ce:section.ce:para: Data generated and/or analyzed during the study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

Grishika Arora: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Nuur Syahidah Sabran: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Chiam-Wen Liew: Resources, Formal analysis. Chai Yan Ng: Supervision, Resources. Foo Wah Low: Supervision. Pramod K. Singh: Writing – review & editing. Hieng Kiat Jun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was supported by the Ministry of Higher Education (MoHE Malaysia), through the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS/1/2022/TK08/UTAR/02/7). Nuur Syahidah Sabran thanks Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman for the provision of the Post-doctoral Research Scholarship.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Baslak, C. et al., 2022. Supercapacitor behaviors of carbon quantum dots by green synthesis method from tea fermented with kombucha. Mater. Sci. Semicond. Process., 147, p. 106738. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mssp.2022.106738.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Sideritis vuralii and its application in supercapacitors
Inorg. Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Başlak, C. et al., 2023. Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Sideritis vuralii and its application in supercapacitors. Inorg. Chem. Commun., 153, p. 110845. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.110845.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-quantum dots derived from denatured milk for efficient chromium-ion sensing and supercapacitor applications
Mater. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Athika, M. et al., 2019. Carbon-quantum dots derived from denatured milk for efficient chromium-ion sensing and supercapacitor applications. Mater. Lett., 241, pp.156–159. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2019.01.064.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon Quantum Dot-Polypyrrole nanocomposite for supercapacitor electrodes
IOP Conf. Ser. Mater. Sci. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mittal, S.S. et al., 2019. Carbon Quantum Dot-Polypyrrole nanocomposite for supercapacitor electrodes. IOP Conf. Ser. Mater. Sci. Eng., 577(1), p. 012194. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1088/1757-899x/577/1/012194.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enriched carbon dots/graphene microfibers towards high-performance micro-supercapacitors
J. Mater. Chem. A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, Q. et al., 2018. Enriched carbon dots/graphene microfibers towards high-performance micro-supercapacitors. J. Mater. Chem. A, 6(29), pp.14112–14119. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1039/c8ta02124d.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in photovoltaic and thermoelectric applications of coupled colloidal quantum dot solids: insights into charge transport fundamentals
Nano Express

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Manna, A. et al., 2024. Recent progress in photovoltaic and thermoelectric applications of coupled colloidal quantum dot solids: insights into charge transport fundamentals. Nano Express, 5(1), p. 012005. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1088/2632-959x/ad2b7e.

Small
